modules.title: Welcome — your transition, your way
modules.html:
Welcome — your transition, your way

You do not have to cut it all off. A transition lets you grow out relaxed or heat-damaged hair to your natural texture while keeping your length — on your own timeline.

This course gives you the one skill that makes or breaks a transition: protecting the fragile line where your two textures meet. You will learn to handle, detangle, style and trim so you keep length instead of snapping it off.

One lesson at a time — validate each to unlock the next.

Read first There is no ‘right’ way to go natural. Big chop, long transition, or somewhere in between — all valid. This course is for keeping your length while you grow.

modules.title: Two textures on one head
modules.html:
Two textures on one head

The moment you stop relaxing or heat-straightening, your natural texture grows in at the roots — while the old, processed hair stays on the ends.

Your new growth is coily, springy and stronger. Your relaxed or heat-damaged ends are straighter, finer, drier and more fragile. They behave differently, drink product differently, and shrink differently — which is exactly why your hair suddenly feels ‘confusing’.

New growth (roots)

Coily, strong, thirsty for moisture, shrinks a lot. This is your real hair arriving.

Processed ends

Straight-ish, weak, dry, prone to splits and single-strand knots. On borrowed time.

Reframe Your ends will eventually go — that’s normal. The goal is to keep them healthy enough to protect your length until you trim them on your terms.

modules.title: The line of demarcation — your fragile zone
modules.html:
The line of demarcation — your fragile zone

Where your two textures meet is called the line of demarcation — and it is the single most fragile point on your head.

One side is strong and coily, the other weak and straight. That junction bends, tugs and snaps easily — especially when it is dry or over-handled. Almost all transition breakage happens right there. Protecting this line is the whole game of transitioning.

  • Keep it moisturised — never detangle or manipulate it dry.
  • Minimise manipulation and tension on it.
  • Balance moisture and protein so it stays flexible, not brittle.
  • Avoid heat, which weakens it even further.
If you remember one thing Baby that line. Every rule in this course exists to protect it.

modules.title: The 5 rules to avoid breakage
modules.html:
The 5 rules to avoid breakage

Transitioning hair breaks for predictable reasons. Follow these five rules and you keep your length.

1

Never detangle dry. Always on soaking-wet, conditioner-slathered hair, ends to roots.

2

Low manipulation. The less you touch it, the less it snaps — choose styles you can leave for days.

3

Moisturise AND strengthen. Moisture for flexibility, a little protein for the weak processed ends.

4

No heat (or almost none). Heat further damages fragile ends and can loosen your new curls.

5

Protect it at night. Satin, hair stretched or twisted so the line is not rubbing and tangling.

The truth about breakage It is almost never random — it is dry detangling, too much manipulation, or heat. Remove those three and your hair thrives.

modules.title: Gentle detangling & handling the line
modules.html:
Gentle detangling & handling the line

Detangling is where transitions are won or lost. Done wrong, you rip through the line; done right, you barely lose a strand.

1

Detangle on wash day only, on soaking-wet hair loaded with slippery conditioner.

2

Work in 4+ sections. Finger-detangle first, then a wide-tooth comb from the ends upward.

3

Go slow at the line — hold each section above and below so you are not tugging the junction.

4

Two textures make two knots: watch for single-strand knots on the straight ends — snip them, don’t yank.

5

Style while still damp so the hair sets smoothly and you are not manipulating it dry later.

New growth

Handles water and detangling well — just be patient with the shrinkage.

Processed ends

Snap easily — give them the gentlest handling, the most slip, the least tension.

If a section fights you Add more conditioner and water — never force a comb through resistance.

modules.title: Moisture + protein balance
modules.html:
Moisture + protein balance

Transitioning hair needs both water (to stay flexible) and protein (to prop up the weak processed ends). Get the balance right and the line stops snapping.

Moisture keeps hair soft and elastic; protein temporarily strengthens damaged, porous hair. Your relaxed or heat-damaged ends are protein-hungry because processing broke down their structure — but too much protein makes hair stiff and brittle. It is a balance, not a contest.

  • Deep-condition for moisture every wash day.
  • Add a light protein treatment every 2–4 weeks — or when hair feels mushy and over-stretchy.
  • Hair hard / straw-like → too much protein, moisturise.
  • Hair limp / gummy / snapping wet → needs protein.
What the line loves A moisture-protein balance: strong enough not to snap, soft enough not to crack. The Recipe course has a strength & softness mask for exactly this.

modules.title: Styles that blend two textures
modules.html:
Styles that blend two textures

The secret to an easy transition is styles that make two textures look like one — so you are not fighting the mismatch every day.

‘Texture-blending’ styles curl the straight ends to match your coily roots, hide the line, and let you go days without manipulation.

  • Twist-outs & braid-outs — curl the ends to match the roots (top pick for blending).
  • Bantu knot-outs, flexi-rod & roller sets — heatless curls that blend beautifully.
  • Braids, twists & buns — low-manipulation protective styles that rest the hair.
  • Wigs & headwraps — full breaks from manipulation while your natural grows.
Best for blending

Twist-outs, braid-outs, bantu knots, roller sets — the ends curl up to match the roots.

Best for resting hair

Braids, twists, buns, wigs — but keep the line moisturised underneath and don’t leave them in too long.

Use the fast-forward button Protective styles speed you up — but don’t ‘set and forget’: moisturise, and take styles down before they tangle at the line.

modules.title: Wash day, nights & morale
modules.html:
Wash day, nights & morale

A calm rhythm keeps the line healthy — and keeps YOU going, because transitioning is as much mental as physical.

1

Pre-poo/oil the ends, cleanse the scalp gently, then deep-condition for moisture.

2

Detangle wet with lots of slip (Lesson 5), apply leave-in, seal, and style damp into a blending style.

3

Midweek: only a light refresh if needed, and re-seal the ends.

At night: satin bonnet or scarf, hair stretched or twisted so the line is not rubbing. Never sleep on loose, dry hair.

On morale 💛 Comparison is the thief of a transition — your chapter 1 is not someone’s chapter 10. Take a photo every month: progress is obvious over months, invisible day to day.

modules.title: The gradual trim & the final cut
modules.html:
The gradual trim & the final cut

You will let go of the processed ends eventually — the only question is all at once, or little by little.

Most transitioners mini-chop: trim a little every 6–10 weeks, phasing out the relaxed ends as the natural grows in. Others transition for a year or more, then do one final big chop. Both are right — your call, your timeline.

  • Trim when ends are splitting, thin, or won’t hold moisture.
  • Dust or trim every 6–10 weeks for a gradual transition.
  • Trim a little more after long protective styles.
  • The better you protect the line, the less you are forced to cut.

The classic mistake: clinging to badly damaged ends for length. If the ends constantly split and snap, they are costing you the healthy length above them — let them go.

There’s freedom on the other side Whenever you cut — mini or big — it is not losing length, it is keeping the healthy hair you grew.

modules.title: Your transition plan + cheat sheet
modules.html:
Your transition plan

A simple rhythm to protect your length and your patience.

  • Weekly: one gentle wash day with a wet detangle + deep condition; a blending style for the week.
  • Every 2–4 weeks: a light protein treatment for the ends; check in on the line.
  • Every 6–10 weeks: dust or trim the weakest ends.
  • Monthly: a progress photo.
  • Always: satin nights, low manipulation, (almost) no heat.

Cheat sheet — “my hair is…”

…breaking at the line

Detangle wetter, with more slip; less manipulation; fix the moisture-protein balance.

…thin or splitting at the ends

Time to trim those — protect the healthy hair above them.

…two textures won’t blend

Wear a twist-out, braid-out or roller set instead of loose hair.

…losing motivation

Monthly photos, a protective style for a few weeks, and come back to this course.

You’ve got this You are not ‘waiting’ to be natural — you already are, at the root. Every month you keep more of what you grow. 💛
